title: Lab Home
subtitle: A practical path from 0 percent placement to grants and infrastructure
summary: A PI-facing entry arguing that a lab home now is the first small decision that makes salary support, DMS collaborations, grant writing, and future infrastructure more plausible.
synopsis: Place Steve Slevinski in a lab now, even at 0 percent if necessary, so that practical work can begin immediately and broader support can grow around that home. The deck keeps the PI ask narrow and disciplined: placement first, broader support second, and the larger infrastructure story only as the consequence of making the right first move.

links.label: NIH Data Management and Sharing Policy
links.description: NIH guidance that supports the case for practical DMS planning and execution help.

links.label: NIMH Data Archive
links.description: Example external repository showing the complexity of real repository delivery work.

slides.title: The First Step
slides.summary: The immediate need is not a mature center. It is a lab home now, because that first placement creates a real base for the work and makes every larger step more credible.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for the immediate timing and opportunity.
bullets: A lab home is needed now
That home creates the base for everything that follows
slides.speech: This conversation is happening now because the immediate need is not a fully built center. It is a lab home. A lab placement gives the work a real university base and makes the next steps far more credible. This deck stays on that first institutional decision.
slides.notes: Open with practical urgency, make the first ask feel modest, and keep the deck narrowly PI-facing.
slides.mermaid:
graph TD
  A[Immediate Need] --> B[Lab Home]
  B --> C[Institutional Base]
  C --> D[Future Growth]

slides.title: What 0 Percent Means
slides.summary: Zero percent support still matters because the first step is affiliation and placement, even if salary support, grants, or donations arrive later.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for the meaning of 0 percent support.
bullets: Funding can grow around the placement
The first ask is smaller than the long-term vision
slides.speech: Starting at 0 percent does not mean the work lacks value. It means the institutional home comes first and the funding structure grows around it. That makes the initial decision smaller, clearer, and more realistic.
slides.notes: Reassure the PI that the initial move is structural, not immediately financial.
slides.image_prompt_concrete: Clean academic diagram showing a small first step labeled lab home leading to larger steps labeled salary support, grants, and infrastructure, minimal white background
slides.image_prompt_abstract: A single foothold growing into a structured pathway of connected platforms, calm strategic visual

slides.title: Why A PI Benefits
slides.summary: A PI benefits because Steve adds practical capacity to the lab through DMS support, workflow cleanup, documentation, code, and repository-facing execution rather than only symbolic affiliation.
bullets: A platform for future grants and collaborations
slides.speech: This is not only an act of generosity. A PI gains practical help with DMS planning, project workflows, repository preparation, and future grant development. The lab becomes a place where useful work starts happening immediately, and the broader operational case can be developed further through Steve Slevinski Value.
slides.notes: Keep the value local to the PI's projects, deadlines, and team needs.
slides.mermaid:
graph LR
  A[Lab Home] --> B[DMS Help]
  A --> C[Repository Support]
  A --> D[Grant Development]
  A --> E[Future Collaborations]

slides.title: What Steve Brings Immediately
slides.summary: What Steve brings now is help in the messy middle between project systems, data organization, technical workflows, standards, and final repository delivery.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for Steve's immediate value.
slides.speech: Steve's immediate value is practical. He can help teams shape a DMS Plan, understand the workflow, organize and validate data, and help carry the project from local systems toward repository delivery.
slides.notes: Describe him as a working collaborator who can make real progress quickly.
slides.image_prompt_concrete: Research operations infographic showing REDCap, spreadsheets, scripts, and repository icons connected by one technical coordinator, professional university style
slides.image_prompt_abstract: Streams of messy data becoming ordered and deliverable through one guiding pathway

slides.title: The Support Lanes
slides.summary: The model works because several support lanes can reinforce each other: lab placement first, direct support where possible, grants under a PI, and complementary gifts or donations.
slides.speech: The plan does not depend on one funding source solving everything immediately. The lab home is first. Partial salary support can come through advocates and collaborators. Grants can be written under the PI. Gifts and donations can provide bridge support and follow-through through that same home.
slides.notes: Make the structure feel coherent, staged, and low-friction.
slides.mermaid:
graph LR
  A[Lab Home] --> B[Partial Salary Support]
  A --> C[Grant Writing Under PI]
  A --> D[Gifts and Donations]
  B --> E[Follow-Through]
  C --> E
  D --> E

slides.title: Why Other University Connections Matter
slides.summary: The lab home is the anchor, but broader University relationships matter because placement in one lab can create outward-facing value across research support, accessibility, and collaboration.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for broader University connections.
bullets: Connect with researchers who need DMS help
Get into code and data workflows
Help teams reach the repository of choice
slides.speech: A lab home is the anchor, but it should not be the only relationship. Steve also wants to connect with others across the University to help with DMS work, get into code and data workflows, support repository delivery, and identify collaborators for future grants.
slides.notes: Help the PI see that saying yes creates institutional leverage, not just private benefit.
slides.image_prompt_concrete: University collaboration network with one lab hub connected to research teams, code workflows, and external repositories, professional academic diagram
slides.image_prompt_abstract: A central node in a university network radiating lines to teams, data systems, and repositories

slides.title: The Grant Path
slides.summary: Once Steve is housed in a lab, the grant story becomes structurally credible because proposals can point to a real base, clear collaborators, and an existing pattern of useful work.
slides.speech: Once there is a lab home, grants can be written under that PI. That makes it possible to fund Steve's role, support DMS and repository work, strengthen software maintenance, and build toward Sutton SignWriting as shared research infrastructure.
slides.notes: Keep the future concrete and tied to the first step.
slides.mermaid:
graph LR
  A[Lab Home] --> B[Grant Writing]
  B --> C[Steve's Role]
  B --> D[DMS and Repository Work]
  B --> E[SignWriting Infrastructure]

slides.title: Gifts And Donations
slides.summary: Gifts and donations can serve as a complementary support lane routed through the PI or institution, but they should strengthen the model rather than replace the core research structure.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for gifts and donations lane.
bullets: Complementary to grants, not a substitute
slides.speech: There is also an altruistic lane through gifts and donations. Routed through the PI, this support can help with bridge funding, follow-through, and infrastructure needs. It should be framed as complementary to grants, not as a replacement for research funding.
slides.notes: Present this as disciplined and secondary, not the central solution.
slides.image_prompt_concrete: Academic funding pathways diagram showing grants as primary lane and gifts and donations as complementary lane, clean institutional style
slides.image_prompt_abstract: Two streams of support feeding one growing structure, one broad and one narrow

slides.title: Low Risk, High Upside
slides.summary: The initial ask is intentionally small, but the upside is strategic because one low-risk placement can unlock collaboration, grants, support pathways, and a real base for the work.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for low-risk high-upside framing.
slides.speech: The strongest way to understand this is as a low-risk, high-upside step. The first decision is simply to provide a lab home. That creates immediate practical value and leaves room for salary support, grants, and infrastructure to grow over time.
slides.notes: This is the calming slide that helps the audience feel the asymmetry of risk and upside.
slides.image_prompt_concrete: Minimalist decision slide showing small input arrow leading to large tree of outcomes, academic and polished
slides.image_prompt_abstract: A small doorway opening into a large structured landscape of future possibilities

slides.title: The Immediate Ask
slides.summary: The ask is straightforward: provide the lab home now so the practical work can continue and the broader support structure can be built from a stable base.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for the immediate ask.
bullets: Place Steve in the lab now
Let funding grow around that home
Use the lab home to build grants, collaborations, and infrastructure
slides.speech: The immediate ask is straightforward: provide the lab home now. That one decision makes it possible to build the rest of the structure around it, including salary support, DMS collaborations, grant writing, and the long-term Sutton SignWriting Infrastructure Initiative.
slides.notes: End with a clean, confident ask and no unnecessary complexity.
slides.mermaid:
graph LR A[Immediate Ask
Lab Home Now] --> B[Real Base] A --> C[Salary Support] A --> D[Grants] A --> E[Collaborations] A --> F[Infrastructure]
